_note: MERGED file. Existing 6 SCOTUS entries from curtilage.citations.EXISTING.json are preserved verbatim under existing_entries. New T2 entries (commercial-REP foundation + state-illustrative) are added under new_entries. Do not drop existing entries.

existing_entries.proposition: Origin of the open-fields doctrine; the Fourth Amendment's protection of 'persons, houses, papers, and effects' does not extend to open fields, and the common law distinguishes open fields from the curtilage.

Reaffirms Hester: an individual has no legitimate expectation that open fields will be free from warrantless intrusion - even fenced land posted with 'No Trespassing' signs - while only the curtilage carries the home's Fourth Amendment protection.

Warrantless naked-eye aerial observation of a fenced backyard within the curtilage, from navigable airspace at 1,000 feet, is not a Fourth Amendment search - observation from a lawful vantage is not entry; supports lawful-vantage vs. lawful-entry distinction.

Establishes the four-factor curtilage test: (1) proximity of the area to the home; (2) whether the area is within an enclosure surrounding the home; (3) the nature of the uses to which the area is put; (4) the steps taken to protect the area from observation by people passing by. Barn ~60 yards from the house and outside the fence held not curtilage.

Curtilage (here, the front porch) is part of the home for Fourth Amendment purposes; the implied license to approach lets a visitor take the front path, knock promptly, wait briefly, and leave ('the Nation's Girl Scouts and trick-or-treaters'). Bringing a drug-detection dog onto the porch exceeded that license and was a trespassory search; decided on the property/trespass theory without reaching Katz.

The automobile exception does not permit the warrantless entry of a home or its curtilage to search a vehicle parked therein; a partially enclosed driveway top abutting the house is curtilage, and the ability to observe curtilage from a lawful vantage is not the right to enter it.

new_entries.case: Dow Chemical Co. v. United States
new_entries.cite: Dow Chemical Co. v. United States, 476 U.S. 227 (1986)
new_entries.holding: An industrial plant complex's outdoor areas between buildings are more like 'open fields' than the curtilage of a home, so EPA's warrantless aerial photography (commercial mapping camera) was not a search. BUT the Court did not say businesses have zero Fourth Amendment privacy - it acknowledged commercial premises retain some protection.
new_entries.point_cited_for: Businesses have no 'curtilage' in the home sense, yet commercial premises still carry some reasonable expectation of privacy

new_entries.cite: See v. City of Seattle, 387 U.S. 541 (1967)
new_entries.holding: Commercial premises are protected by the Fourth Amendment against unreasonable administrative inspections; the businessman, like the homeowner, has a right to insist on a warrant before an administrative entry. (Companion to Camara.)
new_entries.point_cited_for: Commercial premises have Fourth Amendment protection (anchors the 'businesses can have a REP' point)

new_entries.case: G. M. Leasing Corp. v. United States
new_entries.cite: G. M. Leasing Corp. v. United States, 429 U.S. 338 (1977)
new_entries.holding: Warrantless IRS entry into a corporation's offices to seize property for taxes was an unreasonable Fourth Amendment intrusion; business offices not open to the public carry an expectation of privacy. (Seizure of cars from public/open areas was upheld - the line is entry into the private business interior.)
new_entries.point_cited_for: Reinforces commercial-interior REP; warrant needed to enter private business premises

new_entries.cite: State v. Karston, 588 So. 2d 165 (La. Ct. App. 1991)
new_entries.holding: Louisiana intermediate appellate curtilage/search illustration. Decided 1991-10-15 (volume 588 So.2d is 1991), NOT 2008.
new_entries.correction: YEAR CORRECTION: capture said 2008; actual decision date is 1991-10-15 (La. Ct. App.). Reporter 588 So.2d confirms 1991. Use (La. Ct. App. 1991).

new_entries.cite: State v. Weaver, 349 S.W.3d 521 (Tex. Crim. App. 2011)
new_entries.holding: Texas curtilage/search illustration (decided 2011-09-28). Note: court is the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals (the state's highest criminal court), not an intermediate 'Tex. App.' court as the manifest labeled it.
new_entries.correction: COURT LABEL: manifest said 'Tex. App. 2011'; CL shows Texas Court of Criminal Appeals (Tex. Crim. App. 2011). Cite accordingly.

new_entries.cite: State v. Larson, 159 Or. App. 34, 977 P.2d 1175 (1999)
new_entries.holding: Oregon Court of Appeals curtilage/search illustration (decided 1999-03-17).
new_entries.correction: Add parallel cite 977 P.2d 1175 for completeness.

corrections_log: State v. Karston: year 2008 -> 1991 (588 So.2d 165, La. Ct. App., decided 1991-10-15).
State v. Weaver: court 'Tex. App.' -> Texas Court of Criminal Appeals (Tex. Crim. App. 2011).
State v. Larson: added parallel cite 977 P.2d 1175.
